events.description: Israel announced it would raze and occupy 55 towns and villages up to the Litani River.

events.title: "Black Wednesday" — deadliest strikes
events.description: Israel’s "most powerful attacks" killed at least 357 people; Lebanon called it a massacre.

events.title: Huge blast levels Majdal Zoun
events.description: A massive controlled explosion flattened much of the border village, part of the demolition campaign.

conflicts.title: Israel–Iran War
conflicts.description: A direct war between Israel and Iran, fought in two phases. The 12-Day War of June 2025 saw Israel strike Iran's nuclear and military sites, the US bomb Fordow, Natanz and Isfahan, and Iran retaliate against a US base in Qatar before a ceasefire. In February 2026 the US and Israel launched a far larger campaign that killed Supreme Leader Khamenei; Iran blocked the Strait of Hormuz, causing a massive oil shock, and fighting continued into mid-2026.

events.title: Israel launches Operation Rising Lion
events.description: Israel struck Iran's nuclear facilities, military sites and commanders, opening the 12-Day War.

events.title: US bombs Fordow, Natanz and Isfahan
events.description: US B-2s dropped bunker-buster bombs on Iran’s deepest nuclear sites (Operation Midnight Hammer).

events.title: Iran strikes the US base at Al Udeid
events.description: Iran fired missiles at the largest US base in the Middle East in a telegraphed, symbolic response.

events.title: Ceasefire ends the 12-Day War
events.description: A US-announced ceasefire paused the war after twelve days.

events.title: Operation Epic Fury; Khamenei killed
events.description: A massive US-Israeli air campaign killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and hit hundreds of targets.

events.title: Iran retaliates against Israel and US bases
events.description: Iran launched waves of missiles and drones at Israel, Gulf states and US forces.

events.title: Iran blocks the Strait of Hormuz
events.description: Iran closed the strait, triggering the largest oil-supply disruption in history; the US imposed a naval blockade.

events.title: US and Iran agree to cease attacks
events.description: After renewed strikes, the two sides agreed to halt attacks, though the wider standoff remained unresolved.

events.title: Minab school strike kills ~120 children
events.description: A missile destroyed an elementary school in Minab, killing about 156 civilians including some 120 children; investigations attribute the strike to the US.

conflicts.title: US Intervention in Venezuela & Capture of Maduro
conflicts.description: After months of a Caribbean military buildup and strikes on alleged drug-trafficking boats, the US launched 'Operation Absolute Resolve' on 3 January 2026, bombing northern Venezuela and capturing President Nicolás Maduro, who was flown to New York to face narcotrafficking charges. At least 23 Venezuelan and 32 Cuban security personnel were killed. The UN and many states condemned it as a violation of sovereignty and international law.

conflicts.title: Venezuelan Crisis & US Sanctions
conflicts.description: Venezuela's economy collapsed under Maduro amid hyperinflation, shortages, and authoritarian rule, driving over 7 million people to flee — one of the largest displacement crises in the world. The US imposed crushing sanctions (especially on oil) from 2017, which critics say deepened civilian suffering, while backing opposition claims to the presidency.

conflicts.title: US Embargo & Bay of Pigs (Cuba)
conflicts.description: After Cuba's 1959 revolution, the US imposed a comprehensive economic embargo (still in place after 60+ years) and backed the failed 1961 Bay of Pigs invasion by exiles. The 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis brought the world to the brink of nuclear war. The UN General Assembly has voted overwhelmingly against the embargo every year.

conflicts.title: Colombian Armed Conflict (FARC)
conflicts.description: A six-decade war between the Colombian state, leftist guerrillas (FARC, ELN), and right-wing paramilitaries, fueled by the cocaine trade. The US funded the government heavily through 'Plan Colombia.' Around 260,000 died and over 7 million were displaced before the 2016 peace deal with the FARC; the ELN conflict continues.

conflicts.title: Salvadoran Civil War
conflicts.description: A US-backed military government fought leftist FMLN guerrillas, killing around 75,000 — mostly civilians murdered by army death squads. Atrocities included the assassination of Archbishop Óscar Romero (1980) and the El Mozote massacre (1981), in which the US-trained Atlácatl Battalion killed around 1,000 villagers.

conflicts.title: Nicaraguan Contra War
conflicts.description: The US covertly armed and funded the 'Contra' rebels against Nicaragua's leftist Sandinista government, including through the illegal Iran-Contra scheme. The CIA mined Nicaragua's harbours; the World Court ruled against the US in 1986. Around 30,000 died.

conflicts.title: US Invasion of Panama
conflicts.description: The US invaded Panama to depose its former CIA-asset dictator Manuel Noriega on drug charges. Hundreds to possibly thousands of Panamanian civilians died, and the poor El Chorrillo neighbourhood was devastated. The UN General Assembly condemned the invasion as a violation of international law.

conflicts.title: US Occupation of Haiti
conflicts.description: The US militarily occupied Haiti for 19 years, seizing control of its finances, rewriting its constitution to allow foreign land ownership, and reinstating forced-labour ('corvée') road-building. US Marines killed thousands suppressing the Caco rebellion. It was part of a wider pattern of US interventions across the Caribbean.

conflicts.title: Paraguayan War (Triple Alliance)
conflicts.description: The deadliest interstate war in Latin American history. Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay crushed Paraguay, which lost an estimated 60–70% of its population (and up to 90% of its adult men) to combat, disease, and famine. Paraguay was left devastated and stripped of territory.

conflicts.title: War of the Pacific
conflicts.description: Chile defeated Peru and Bolivia in a war over nitrate-rich Atacama territory. Chile annexed land that left Bolivia permanently landlocked — a grievance Bolivia still raises today. It reshaped the borders and economies of the southern Andes.

conflicts.title: Mexican Revolution
conflicts.description: A decade of revolution and civil war that overthrew the Díaz dictatorship and reshaped Mexico, with figures like Zapata and Pancho Villa. An estimated 1–2 million people died. The US intervened militarily several times during the upheaval.

conflicts.title: Peru — Shining Path Conflict
conflicts.description: The Maoist Shining Path insurgency and the state's counterinsurgency killed an estimated 70,000 Peruvians, most of them indigenous Quechua-speaking peasants caught between both sides. A truth commission documented massacres and forced disappearances by both the guerrillas and the military.

conflicts.title: Brazilian Military Dictatorship
conflicts.description: A US-backed military coup overthrew Brazil's elected government, ushering in 21 years of dictatorship that tortured tens of thousands and killed or disappeared hundreds of opponents. It was a pillar of the regional Operation Condor network.

conflicts.title: Taiping Rebellion
conflicts.description: A massive civil war in Qing China led by a millenarian movement that established a rival kingdom. It is one of the deadliest conflicts in all of human history, with an estimated 20–30 million dead, mostly from famine and disease. Britain and France ultimately backed the Qing to crush it.

events.title: Rebellion proclaimed at Jintian
events.description: A millenarian movement rose against the Qing dynasty.

events.title: Taiping capture Nanjing
events.description: The rebels made Nanjing the capital of their Heavenly Kingdom.

events.title: Fall of Nanjing
events.description: The rebellion collapsed after perhaps 20–30 million deaths — one of history’s deadliest wars.

conflicts.title: First Sino-Japanese War
conflicts.description: Japan defeated Qing China, seizing Taiwan and dominance over Korea. The war announced Japan's arrival as an imperial power and accelerated the decline of imperial China, feeding into its 'Century of Humiliation.'

events.title: Battle of the Yalu River
events.description: Japan destroyed the Chinese Beiyang Fleet, seizing naval dominance.

events.title: Treaty of Shimonoseki
events.description: China ceded Taiwan and recognised Korean independence.

conflicts.title: Boxer Rebellion & Eight-Nation Intervention
conflicts.description: An anti-foreign, anti-Christian uprising in China was crushed by an Eight-Nation Alliance (Britain, France, Germany, Russia, Japan, the US, Italy, Austria-Hungary), which looted Beijing and imposed a crushing indemnity on China. It deepened foreign domination of China.

events.title: Siege of the international legations
events.description: Boxers and imperial troops besieged the foreign quarter for 55 days.

events.title: Eight-Nation Alliance takes Beijing
events.description: Foreign armies relieved the siege and looted the capital.

conflicts.title: Japanese Colonization of Korea
conflicts.description: Japan annexed and ruled Korea for 35 years, suppressing Korean language and culture, conscripting forced labour, and forcing up to 200,000 women (mostly Korean) into sexual slavery as wartime 'comfort women.' The legacy still poisons Japan–Korea relations.

events.title: Japan annexes Korea
events.description: Korea was absorbed into the Japanese Empire for 35 years.

events.title: March 1st independence movement
events.description: Mass protests for independence were violently suppressed.

events.title: Liberation of Korea
events.description: Japan's defeat freed Korea, which was then divided.

conflicts.title: Chinese Civil War
conflicts.description: The war between Mao's Communists and Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalists (paused to fight Japan) killed several million. The Communist victory in 1949 founded the People's Republic of China; the Nationalists fled to Taiwan, creating the still-unresolved cross-strait division.

events.title: Shanghai massacre of communists
events.description: The Nationalist purge of communists opened the civil war.

events.title: The Long March begins
events.description: The Red Army’s epic retreat became the founding myth of Maoism.

events.title: Communist victory; PRC founded
events.description: Mao proclaimed the People’s Republic as the Nationalists fled to Taiwan.

conflicts.title: Tiananmen Square Massacre
conflicts.description: The Chinese military crushed peaceful pro-democracy protests in Beijing, killing hundreds to possibly thousands of demonstrators. China censors all mention of it to this day. The 'Tank Man' image became a global symbol of defiance.

events.title: Protests fill Tiananmen Square
events.description: Weeks of pro-democracy demonstrations gathered in the heart of Beijing.

events.title: Military crushes the protests
events.description: Troops and tanks killed hundreds to thousands of civilians.

conflicts.title: Laotian Civil War (US Secret War)
conflicts.description: During the Vietnam War the US dropped over 2 million tons of bombs on Laos — making it the most heavily bombed country per capita in history. Vast unexploded ordnance still kills and maims Laotians decades later. The CIA ran a 'secret army' of Hmong fighters.

events.title: US secret bombing of Laos begins
events.description: Laos became the most heavily bombed country per capita in history.

events.title: Pathet Lao take power
events.description: Communist forces won as the US left Indochina.

conflicts.title: Malayan Emergency
conflicts.description: Britain fought communist insurgents in colonial Malaya, pioneering counterinsurgency tactics including the forced resettlement of 500,000 people into guarded 'new villages.' British forces committed atrocities such as the Batang Kali massacre (1948).

conflicts.title: Myanmar Civil War (Post-Coup)
conflicts.description: After the military (Tatmadaw) seized power in a February 2021 coup, mass protests were met with lethal force, igniting a nationwide civil war. The junta has conducted airstrikes, mass killings, and village burnings; thousands are dead and millions displaced. Ethnic armed groups and a resistance movement control much of the country.

events.title: Military coup
events.description: The army seized power, reversing a decade of tentative democracy.

events.title: Crackdown on protesters
events.description: Security forces killed hundreds as resistance spread nationwide.

events.title: Resistance offensive (Operation 1027)
events.description: An ethnic-alliance offensive seized swathes of territory from the junta.

conflicts.title: 1953 Iranian Coup (Mossadegh)
conflicts.description: The CIA and British MI6 orchestrated a coup that overthrew Iran's elected PM Mohammad Mossadegh after he nationalized the British-controlled oil industry. They reinstalled the autocratic Shah, whose repressive rule fed the 1979 revolution and lasting Iranian distrust of the West.

events.title: CIA/MI6 coup topples Mossadegh
events.description: Britain and the US overthrew Iran's elected PM over oil nationalisation.

conflicts.title: Iranian Revolution
conflicts.description: A mass revolution overthrew the US-backed Shah and established Ayatollah Khomeini's Islamic Republic. It reshaped the Middle East, triggered the US embassy hostage crisis, and made Iran and the US enduring adversaries.

events.title: Black Friday shootings
events.description: Troops fired on protesters, a turning point against the Shah.

events.title: The Shah flees Iran
events.description: The monarch left as the revolution reached its climax.

events.title: Khomeini returns; monarchy falls
events.description: An Islamic Republic replaced 2,500 years of monarchy.

conflicts.title: British Occupation of Egypt
conflicts.description: Britain occupied Egypt to secure the Suez Canal and its route to India, controlling the country for over 70 years. British forces suppressed nationalist uprisings (1919, 1952) until the 1952 revolution and the 1956 Suez Crisis ended British dominance.

conflicts.title: Mahdist War (Sudan)
conflicts.description: A Sudanese Islamic uprising created the Mahdist State, which Britain and Egypt eventually crushed. At Omdurman (1898), British machine guns killed around 10,000 Sudanese in hours. The reconquest opened Sudan to Anglo-Egyptian colonial rule.

events.title: Fall of Khartoum
events.description: Mahdist forces took the city and killed General Gordon.

events.title: Battle of Omdurman
events.description: Anglo-Egyptian machine guns destroyed the Mahdist army.

conflicts.title: Hama Massacre (Syria)
conflicts.description: Hafez al-Assad's regime crushed a Muslim Brotherhood uprising in the Syrian city of Hama, killing an estimated 10,000–40,000 people and razing much of the old city. It was one of the deadliest single acts of state repression in the modern Middle East.

events.title: Syrian army crushes Hama
events.description: Hafez al-Assad's forces killed tens of thousands to crush an uprising.

conflicts.title: First Intifada
conflicts.description: A Palestinian popular uprising against the Israeli occupation, marked by mass protests, strikes, and stone-throwing met with Israel's 'force, might and beatings' policy. Around 1,200 Palestinians and 160 Israelis died. It led to the Oslo peace process.

events.title: Uprising begins in Gaza
events.description: A largely grassroots Palestinian uprising against the occupation.

events.title: Oslo Accords
events.description: The accords set up limited Palestinian self-rule.

conflicts.title: Second Intifada
conflicts.description: A far more violent Palestinian uprising involving suicide bombings and heavy Israeli military operations. Around 3,000 Palestinians and 1,000 Israelis died. Israel built the West Bank separation barrier and reoccupied Palestinian cities.

events.title: Uprising erupts
events.description: A far more violent uprising broke out after failed peace talks.

events.title: Israeli reoccupation of the West Bank
events.description: Operation Defensive Shield followed a wave of suicide bombings.

events.title: Ceasefire ends the uprising
events.description: Around 3,000 Palestinians and 1,000 Israelis died over five years.

conflicts.title: Egyptian Coup & Rabaa Massacre
conflicts.description: After a military coup ousted Egypt's first elected president Mohamed Morsi, security forces massacred at least 800–1,000 protesters in a single day at Cairo's Rabaa Square — one of the largest killings of demonstrators in modern history. It entrenched Sisi's authoritarian rule.

events.title: Military coup ousts Morsi
events.description: The army removed Egypt's first elected president.

events.title: Rabaa massacre
events.description: Security forces killed over 800 protesters in a single day.

conflicts.title: Boer Wars
conflicts.description: Britain fought the Dutch-descended Boer republics for control of gold- and diamond-rich South Africa. Britain interned Boer and African civilians in concentration camps where around 28,000 Boers (mostly children) and many thousands of Africans died of disease and starvation — among the first modern concentration camps.

events.title: Second Boer War begins
events.description: Britain fought the Boer republics for control of gold-rich South Africa.

events.title: British concentration camps
events.description: Tens of thousands of Boer and African civilians died in the camps.

events.title: Treaty of Vereeniging
events.description: The Boer republics were absorbed into the British Empire.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Zulu War
conflicts.description: Britain invaded the independent Zulu Kingdom in South Africa. Despite the Zulu victory at Isandlwana, Britain ultimately destroyed the kingdom and absorbed it — a classic episode of the 'Scramble for Africa.'

events.title: Battle of Isandlwana
events.description: The Zulu inflicted a stunning defeat on a British column.

events.title: Battle of Ulundi
events.description: Britain destroyed the Zulu army and annexed the kingdom.

conflicts.title: Ethiopian Red Terror (Derg)
conflicts.description: The Marxist Derg junta under Mengistu unleashed a campaign of mass killing against opponents, killing an estimated 500,000. Bodies were left in the streets and families charged for the bullets. Soviet- and Cuban-backed, the regime also presided over the 1983–85 famine.

events.title: The Derg's Red Terror
events.description: The military junta killed tens of thousands of suspected opponents.

conflicts.title: Idi Amin's Uganda
conflicts.description: Ugandan dictator Idi Amin killed an estimated 300,000–500,000 people, expelled the country's Asian minority, and ruled by terror. Tanzania invaded in 1979 to overthrow him after he tried to annex Tanzanian territory.

events.title: Idi Amin seizes power
events.description: A coup began eight years of dictatorship and mass killing.

events.title: Expulsion of Ugandan Asians
events.description: Some 80,000 South Asians were expelled, wrecking the economy.

events.title: Amin overthrown
events.description: A Tanzanian invasion ended a regime that killed up to 500,000.

conflicts.title: Lord's Resistance Army Insurgency
conflicts.description: Joseph Kony's LRA waged a brutal insurgency across Uganda, South Sudan, DR Congo, and CAR, abducting an estimated 60,000–100,000 children as soldiers and sex slaves and displacing millions. Kony remains at large; the ICC indicted him in 2005.

events.title: Lord's Resistance Army insurgency begins
events.description: The LRA waged a brutal war marked by child abduction.

events.title: ICC issues warrants for LRA leaders
events.description: Joseph Kony became one of the world’s most wanted men.

conflicts.title: Burundian Genocides
conflicts.description: Burundi suffered cycles of ethnic mass killing between Hutu and Tutsi: the 1972 genocide of Hutus (an estimated 100,000–300,000 dead) and the 1993 violence that killed around 50,000–100,000 and helped ignite the wider Great Lakes crisis alongside Rwanda.

events.title: Mass killings of Hutu
events.description: The army killed an estimated 100,000–300,000 Hutu.

events.title: Assassination sparks new killings
events.description: The murder of the elected president triggered mass ethnic violence.

conflicts.title: Mozambican Civil War
conflicts.description: After independence from Portugal, Mozambique's Soviet-aligned FRELIMO government fought the RENAMO rebels backed by white-ruled Rhodesia and apartheid South Africa. Around 1 million died, many from war-induced famine, and the country was littered with landmines.

events.title: RENAMO insurgency begins
events.description: An externally-backed insurgency devastated the newly independent state.

events.title: Rome General Peace Accords
events.description: The war ended after around a million deaths.

conflicts.title: Central African Republic Civil War
conflicts.description: A civil war between the mainly Muslim Séléka and Christian anti-Balaka militias has driven sectarian massacres and displaced a quarter of the population. Russia's Wagner Group props up the government in exchange for mining rights.

conflicts.title: Ogaden War
conflicts.description: Somalia invaded Ethiopia's Somali-inhabited Ogaden region. In a dramatic Cold War switch, the USSR and Cuba backed Ethiopia, sending thousands of Cuban troops to repel Somalia. Tens of thousands died.

events.title: Somalia invades the Ogaden
events.description: Somalia attacked to seize the ethnic-Somali Ogaden region.

events.title: Ethiopia repels the invasion
events.description: Soviet arms and Cuban troops helped Ethiopia win.

conflicts.title: Zimbabwe — Gukurahundi Massacres
conflicts.description: Robert Mugabe's North Korean-trained Fifth Brigade massacred an estimated 20,000 Ndebele civilians in Matabeleland to crush political rivals. Zimbabwe has never fully acknowledged the killings.

events.title: Gukurahundi massacres in Matabeleland
events.description: A North Korea-trained brigade killed an estimated 20,000 Ndebele.

events.title: Unity Accord
events.description: A political merger ended the campaign of violence.

conflicts.title: Cameroon Anglophone Crisis
conflicts.description: Cameroon's English-speaking minority rose against marginalization by the Francophone government, escalating into an armed separatist conflict ('Ambazonia'). Government forces and separatists have both committed atrocities; thousands are dead and over a million displaced.

conflicts.title: Malagasy Uprising
conflicts.description: France brutally crushed a nationalist uprising in its colony of Madagascar, killing an estimated 30,000–100,000 Malagasy through massacres, torture, and 'death flights' in which prisoners were thrown from aircraft. It is one of the lesser-known atrocities of French colonialism.

events.title: Malagasy uprising against French rule
events.description: A nationalist revolt spread across the island.

events.title: French repression
events.description: French forces killed tens of thousands crushing the revolt.

conflicts.title: Crimean War
conflicts.description: Britain, France, and the Ottoman Empire fought Russia over influence in the declining Ottoman lands. Around 500,000 died, mostly from disease — a scandal that launched Florence Nightingale's nursing reforms. It checked Russian expansion toward the Mediterranean.

events.title: Siege of Sevastopol
events.description: An 11-month siege of the Russian naval base decided the war.

events.title: Battle of Balaclava
events.description: Site of the doomed Charge of the Light Brigade.

events.title: Treaty of Paris
events.description: The first war widely covered by photography and the telegraph.

conflicts.title: Franco-Prussian War
conflicts.description: Prussia's decisive defeat of France unified the German states into the German Empire and seized Alsace-Lorraine. The humiliation seeded French revanchism that helped fuel World War I.

events.title: Battle of Sedan
events.description: The capture of Napoleon III's army decided the war.

events.title: Siege of Paris
events.description: Paris held out for four months amid starvation.

events.title: German Empire proclaimed
events.description: Germany was unified in the Hall of Mirrors after victory.

conflicts.title: Balkan Wars
conflicts.description: Two wars in which Balkan states drove the Ottoman Empire out of most of Europe, then fought each other over the spoils. Marked by ethnic cleansing and atrocities, they destabilized the region and were a direct prelude to World War I.

events.title: First Balkan War
events.description: A Balkan league drove the Ottomans almost entirely out of Europe.

events.title: Second Balkan War
events.description: The victors fought over the spoils, sowing seeds for WWI.

conflicts.title: Soviet Invasion of Czechoslovakia
conflicts.description: The USSR led a Warsaw Pact invasion to crush the 'Prague Spring' liberalization. Around 500,000 troops occupied Czechoslovakia, ending reform and reasserting Soviet control over Eastern Europe for another two decades.

events.title: Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia
events.description: Soviet-led forces crushed the "socialism with a human face" reforms.

conflicts.title: Croatian War of Independence
conflicts.description: As Yugoslavia broke up, Croatia fought Serb forces seeking to carve out Serb-controlled territory. The war featured ethnic cleansing, the destruction of Vukovar, and the shelling of Dubrovnik. Around 20,000 died before Croatia retook its territory in 1995.

events.title: Battle of Vukovar
events.description: An 87-day siege razed the city and was followed by a massacre.

events.title: Operation Storm
events.description: A Croatian offensive ended the war and displaced most local Serbs.

conflicts.title: Tajikistani Civil War
conflicts.description: A civil war erupted in newly independent Tajikistan between the government and an opposition of democratic and Islamist forces. Around 50,000–100,000 died and over a million were displaced; Russia backed the government militarily.

events.title: Civil war erupts
events.description: A brutal war killed tens of thousands after independence.

events.title: Peace agreement
events.description: A power-sharing deal ended the war.

conflicts.title: Transnistria War (Moldova)
conflicts.description: When Moldova became independent, the Russian-speaking Transnistria region broke away with the backing of Russia's 14th Army. Russia still stations troops there, propping up an unrecognized breakaway state — a 'frozen conflict' like those in Georgia and Ukraine.

events.title: War in Transnistria
events.description: A Russian-backed strip of Moldova fought for separation.

events.title: Ceasefire freezes the conflict
events.description: Russian troops remain in a frozen breakaway region.

conflicts.title: Indo-Pakistani War of 1965
conflicts.description: India and Pakistan fought a second war over Kashmir, the largest tank battles since WW2. Thousands died before a UN ceasefire and Soviet-brokered peace restored the pre-war lines, leaving Kashmir unresolved.

conflicts.title: Operation Blue Star & Anti-Sikh Pogrom
conflicts.description: India's army stormed the Golden Temple, Sikhism's holiest site, to flush out militants, killing hundreds. After PM Indira Gandhi's assassination by her Sikh bodyguards, anti-Sikh pogroms killed an estimated 3,000+ Sikhs in Delhi, often with police complicity.

events.title: Operation Blue Star
events.description: The army stormed the Golden Temple, killing militants and pilgrims.

events.title: Anti-Sikh pogrom
events.description: After Indira Gandhi's assassination, mobs killed thousands of Sikhs.

conflicts.title: Nepalese Civil War
conflicts.description: A Maoist insurgency against Nepal's monarchy killed around 17,000. The war ended with a peace deal that abolished the 240-year-old monarchy in 2008 and turned Nepal into a federal republic.

events.title: Maoist People's War begins
events.description: A Maoist insurgency challenged the monarchy.

events.title: Royal palace massacre
events.description: The crown prince killed most of the royal family.

events.title: Comprehensive Peace Accord
events.description: The war ended and Nepal became a republic.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Afghan Wars
conflicts.description: Britain invaded Afghanistan three times during the 'Great Game' rivalry with Russia. The catastrophic 1842 retreat from Kabul annihilated a British army. Afghanistan earned its reputation as the 'graveyard of empires,' resisting British control and winning full independence in 1919.

events.title: Retreat from Kabul
events.description: An entire British-Indian army was annihilated in the passes.

events.title: Battle of Maiwand
events.description: A famous Afghan victory in the Second Anglo-Afghan War.

conflicts.title: New Zealand Land Wars
conflicts.description: British colonial forces fought the indigenous Māori over sovereignty and the mass confiscation of Māori land in breach of the Treaty of Waitangi. The wars and confiscations dispossessed Māori and entrenched British settler control of New Zealand.

conflicts.title: Sengoku Period (Japan's Warring States)
conflicts.description: Nearly 150 years of near-constant civil war between rival samurai warlords (daimyo) fractured Japan. It ended with the country's reunification under Tokugawa Ieyasu, who founded a shogunate that ruled for over 250 years.

conflicts.title: Eighty Years' War (Dutch Revolt)
conflicts.description: The Protestant Low Countries rebelled against the rule of Catholic Habsburg Spain. After eighty years of war, including the sack of Antwerp, the Dutch Republic won independence and became a global trading and colonial power.

events.title: Capture of Brielle sparks the revolt
events.description: The Sea Beggars’ raid ignited open rebellion against Spain.

events.title: Peace of Münster
events.description: Spain recognised Dutch independence after 80 years.

conflicts.title: Thirty Years' War
conflicts.description: A catastrophic religious and dynastic war fought largely on German soil, drawing in Spain, France, Sweden, and Austria. An estimated 4.5–8 million died, and some German regions lost a third to half their population. The Peace of Westphalia (1648) founded the modern system of sovereign states.

events.title: Defenestration of Prague
events.description: Bohemian nobles threw imperial officials from a window, sparking the war.

events.title: Battle of White Mountain
events.description: A Catholic victory crushed the Bohemian revolt.

events.title: Sack of Magdeburg
events.description: Imperial troops massacred around 20,000 — one of the war’s worst atrocities.

events.title: Peace of Westphalia
events.description: The peace founded the modern system of sovereign states.

conflicts.title: Qing Conquest of China
conflicts.description: The Manchu-led Qing overthrew the Ming dynasty and conquered China, with massacres such as the Yangzhou massacre (1645). Decades of war and upheaval killed an estimated 25 million. The Qing forced Han Chinese men to wear the queue hairstyle on pain of death.

events.title: Manchus capture Beijing
events.description: The Qing dynasty replaced the Ming and began conquering China.

events.title: Yangzhou massacre
events.description: Qing troops massacred the city over ten days.

conflicts.title: English Civil War
conflicts.description: War between Parliamentarians and Royalists ended with the execution of King Charles I (1649) and a brief republic under Oliver Cromwell. Proportionally one of Britain's deadliest conflicts, it reshaped the balance between crown and parliament.

events.title: Battle of Edgehill
events.description: The first pitched battle between King and Parliament.

events.title: Battle of Naseby
events.description: Parliament's New Model Army broke the Royalist cause.

events.title: Execution of Charles I
events.description: The king was beheaded and England briefly became a republic.

conflicts.title: Cromwellian Conquest of Ireland
conflicts.description: Cromwell's brutal conquest of Ireland, including the massacres at Drogheda and Wexford, mass land confiscation, and famine, killed an estimated 15–40% of the Irish population. Thousands were shipped to the Caribbean as indentured labour.

events.title: Siege of Drogheda
events.description: Cromwell's forces massacred the garrison and many civilians.

events.title: Act for the Settlement of Ireland
events.description: Mass land confiscation and death reshaped Ireland.

conflicts.title: Ottoman Wars in Europe
conflicts.description: The Ottoman Empire pushed deep into Europe, twice besieging Vienna (1529, 1683). The failed 1683 siege, broken by a Polish-led relief, marked the high tide of Ottoman expansion and the start of its long retreat from Central Europe.

conflicts.title: Mughal Empire Expansion
conflicts.description: The Mughals conquered and ruled most of the Indian subcontinent, one of the wealthiest empires of its age. Expansion under Aurangzeb included long, bloody wars against the Marathas and others before the empire's slow decline opened the door to British colonization.

conflicts.title: War of the Spanish Succession
conflicts.description: A Europe-wide war over who would inherit the Spanish throne, pitting France and Spain against an alliance of Britain, Austria, and the Dutch. Around 400,000+ died; Britain gained Gibraltar and rising naval dominance.

events.title: Battle of Blenheim
events.description: Marlborough's victory checked French dominance.

events.title: Treaty of Utrecht
events.description: The settlement redrew Europe and gave Britain Gibraltar.

conflicts.title: Great Northern War
conflicts.description: A coalition led by Russia broke the dominance of the Swedish Empire in northern Europe. Russia's victory under Peter the Great made it a major European power and gave it its Baltic coast, where it founded St. Petersburg.

events.title: Battle of Poltava
events.description: Russia crushed Sweden's army, ending its era as a great power.

events.title: Treaty of Nystad
events.description: Russia gained the Baltic coast and became a European power.

conflicts.title: Dzungar Genocide
conflicts.description: Qing China exterminated the Dzungar Mongols of Central Asia, killing an estimated 80% of the population (around 500,000–800,000) through massacre, famine, and disease. The Qianlong Emperor ordered the destruction; the emptied land was resettled by other peoples. It is one of the clearest genocides of the 18th century.

events.title: Qing conquest of the Dzungars
events.description: The Qing destroyed the Dzungar Khanate.

events.title: Extermination of the Dzungar people
events.description: Perhaps 80% of the Dzungars were killed — a deliberate genocide.

conflicts.title: Seven Years' War
conflicts.description: Often called the first true world war, fought across Europe, the Americas, India, and the seas. Britain and Prussia defeated France, Austria, and allies; around 1 million died. Britain seized Canada and dominance in India, while war debts helped trigger the American and French revolutions.

events.title: Battle of Plassey
events.description: The victory gave the East India Company control of Bengal.

events.title: Battle of Quebec
events.description: Britain took Quebec, deciding the fate of French North America.

events.title: Treaty of Paris
events.description: France ceded Canada and much of its empire to Britain.

conflicts.title: British Conquest of India (Mysore & Maratha Wars)
conflicts.description: Through the Anglo-Mysore and Anglo-Maratha wars, the British East India Company defeated the major Indian powers and seized control of most of the subcontinent, paving the way for the British Raj.

conflicts.title: American Revolutionary War
conflicts.description: The thirteen American colonies won independence from Britain, aided decisively by France. It created the United States and inspired revolutionary movements worldwide, though it left slavery and the dispossession of Native Americans intact.

events.title: Battles of Lexington and Concord
events.description: The "shot heard round the world" began the war.

events.title: Declaration of Independence
events.description: The thirteen colonies declared independence from Britain.

events.title: Surrender at Yorktown
events.description: A Franco-American victory effectively won the war.

conflicts.title: French Revolution & Reign of Terror
conflicts.description: The revolution overthrew the French monarchy and proclaimed the Rights of Man, but descended into the Reign of Terror, in which tens of thousands were executed by guillotine. The brutal suppression of the Vendée uprising killed up to 170,000 and is sometimes called a genocide.

events.title: Storming of the Bastille
events.description: The fall of the royal prison became the symbol of the revolution.

events.title: Execution of Louis XVI
events.description: The king was guillotined, radicalising the revolution.

events.title: Reign of Terror
events.description: Tens of thousands were executed as enemies of the revolution.

events.title: Napoleon seizes power
events.description: The coup of 18 Brumaire ended the revolution.

conflicts.title: Haitian Revolution
conflicts.description: The only successful large-scale slave revolt in history: enslaved Africans in the French colony of Saint-Domingue overthrew their masters and defeated Napoleon's army to found Haiti, the first Black republic. France later punished it with a crippling 'independence debt.'

events.title: Slave uprising begins
events.description: The largest slave revolt in history erupted in the French colony.

events.title: French expedition to restore slavery
events.description: Napoleon sent an army that was defeated by resistance and yellow fever.

events.title: Haiti declares independence
events.description: Haiti became the first state founded by a successful slave revolt.

conflicts.title: Napoleonic Wars
conflicts.description: Napoleon's France fought a series of coalitions across Europe, briefly dominating the continent before his catastrophic 1812 invasion of Russia and final defeat at Waterloo (1815). An estimated 3.5–6 million died. The wars redrew Europe and spread revolutionary ideas and nationalism.

events.title: Battle of Trafalgar
events.description: British naval victory secured control of the seas for a century.

events.title: Battle of Austerlitz
events.description: Napoleon's masterpiece crushed the Austro-Russian armies.

events.title: French invasion of Russia
events.description: Napoleon's Grande Armée was destroyed by battle, cold and hunger.

events.title: Battle of Waterloo
events.description: Napoleon's final defeat ended two decades of war.

conflicts.title: Russo-Turkish Wars
conflicts.description: A long series of wars in which an expanding Russia steadily pushed back the declining Ottoman Empire, gaining the Black Sea coast and Caucasus and backing Balkan independence movements. The conflicts fed the 'Eastern Question' that destabilized 19th-century Europe.

conflicts.title: Mexican–American War
conflicts.description: The US invaded Mexico and seized roughly half its territory — today's California, Texas, Arizona, New Mexico, and more. It fulfilled US 'Manifest Destiny' expansion and left a lasting grievance over the lost lands.

events.title: US declares war on Mexico
events.description: A border dispute became a war of continental expansion.

events.title: Fall of Mexico City
events.description: US forces stormed Chapultepec and took the capital.

events.title: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo
events.description: Mexico ceded nearly half its territory, including California.

conflicts.title: Circassian Genocide
conflicts.description: As Russia completed its conquest of the Caucasus, it expelled and killed the Circassian people. An estimated 600,000–1.5 million died or were driven into the Ottoman Empire, with mass deaths during forced sea crossings. Russia repopulated the emptied lands.

events.title: Mass expulsion of the Circassians
events.description: Russia expelled most Circassians; hundreds of thousands died.

conflicts.title: American Civil War
conflicts.description: The war between the Union and the secessionist Confederacy over slavery and states' rights killed an estimated 620,000–750,000 — the deadliest war in US history. The Union victory ended slavery in the United States.

events.title: Confederates fire on Fort Sumter
events.description: The bombardment of a federal fort opened the American Civil War.

events.title: Emancipation Proclamation
events.description: Lincoln declared enslaved people in rebel states to be free.

events.title: Battle of Gettysburg
events.description: The bloodiest battle of the war turned the tide against the Confederacy.

events.title: Surrender at Appomattox
events.description: Lee's surrender effectively ended a war that killed some 620,000.

conflicts.title: Taiwan Strait Tensions
conflicts.description: After losing the Chinese Civil War in 1949, the Nationalists fled to Taiwan, which has governed itself ever since while China (PRC) claims it as a breakaway province and vows 'reunification', by force if necessary. China stages large military drills and incursions; the US is Taiwan's main arms supplier and security backer under deliberate 'strategic ambiguity'. A Chinese invasion is considered one of the most dangerous potential flashpoints of the 21st century.

conflicts.title: Falklands War
conflicts.description: Argentina's military junta invaded the British-held Falkland Islands (Malvinas); Britain sent a naval task force 8,000 miles to retake them in a 10-week war that killed about 900. The defeat hastened the fall of Argentina's dictatorship. The islands' sovereignty remains disputed.

events.title: Argentina invades the Falklands
events.description: The junta seized the British-held islands, provoking a naval war.

events.title: Sinking of the Belgrano
events.description: A British submarine sank the cruiser, killing 323.

events.title: Argentine surrender
events.description: Britain retook the islands; the defeat toppled the junta.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Ashanti Wars
conflicts.description: A series of wars over the 19th century in which Britain conquered the powerful Ashanti Empire in West Africa (today Ghana), looting Kumasi and exiling the Asantehene. It was part of the wider 'Scramble for Africa' and Britain's Gold Coast colony.

conflicts.title: Anglo-Burmese Wars
conflicts.description: Three wars through which Britain conquered and annexed all of Burma (Myanmar), exiling its last king and ruling it as a province of British India. The conquest extracted teak, oil, and rubies, and the borders it drew helped fuel Myanmar's later ethnic conflicts.

events.title: First Anglo-Burmese War
events.description: Britain began the piecemeal conquest of Burma.

events.title: Britain annexes Burma
events.description: The last Burmese king was deposed and exiled.

conflicts.title: Amritsar (Jallianwala Bagh) Massacre
conflicts.description: British troops under General Dyer opened fire without warning on thousands of unarmed Indians trapped in an enclosed garden in Amritsar, killing several hundred to ~1,000 and wounding over a thousand. The massacre became a turning point that galvanized the Indian independence movement.

events.title: Jallianwala Bagh massacre
events.description: British troops fired on a peaceful crowd, killing hundreds.

conflicts.title: Cyprus Emergency (EOKA)
conflicts.description: Greek Cypriot EOKA guerrillas fought a campaign against British colonial rule seeking union with Greece. Britain used detention camps and hangings to suppress it. Cyprus gained independence in 1960 — but unresolved tensions led to the 1974 Turkish invasion and the island's division.

events.title: EOKA insurgency against British rule
events.description: Greek Cypriots fought for union with Greece.

events.title: Independence agreement
events.description: Cyprus became independent under a power-sharing constitution.

conflicts.title: Rhodesian Bush War
conflicts.description: After white-minority Rhodesia declared independence to preserve white rule, Black nationalist guerrillas (ZANU and ZAPU) fought a 15-year liberation war. Around 20,000+ died before majority rule and the creation of Zimbabwe in 1980 under Robert Mugabe.

events.title: White-minority government declares UDI
events.description: Rhodesia’s unilateral independence entrenched white-minority rule.

events.title: Lancaster House Agreement
events.description: The settlement led to majority rule and an independent Zimbabwe.

conflicts.title: French Mandate of Syria & Lebanon
conflicts.description: After WW1, France took control of Syria and Lebanon, crushing the Arab Kingdom of Syria and suppressing the Great Syrian Revolt (1925–27) — including the aerial bombardment of Damascus. France's divide-and-rule policies and the borders it drew (carving out Lebanon) shaped the region's later sectarian conflicts.

conflicts.title: Cameroon — Bamileke War (Hidden War)
conflicts.description: As Cameroon moved to independence, France waged a brutal secret war against the UPC independence movement, especially among the Bamileke and Bassa peoples. French forces used napalm, mass killings, and severed heads on display; estimates of the dead range from tens of thousands to several hundred thousand. It remains one of the least-known atrocities of French decolonization.

conflicts.title: French Wars of Religion
conflicts.description: Decades of civil war between French Catholics and Protestant Huguenots killed an estimated 2–4 million. The St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre (1572) saw thousands of Huguenots slaughtered in Paris and beyond. The wars ended with the Edict of Nantes granting limited tolerance.

events.title: St Bartholomew's Day Massacre
events.description: Thousands of Protestant Huguenots were slaughtered across France.

events.title: Edict of Nantes
events.description: The edict granted the Huguenots toleration, ending the wars.

conflicts.title: Spanish–American War
conflicts.description: The US defeated Spain and seized its remaining empire — taking Puerto Rico, Guam, and the Philippines, and making Cuba a protectorate. It marked the United States' emergence as an overseas imperial power and led directly to the brutal Philippine–American War.

events.title: Sinking of the USS Maine
events.description: The explosion pushed the US toward war with Spain.

events.title: Battle of Manila Bay
events.description: The US destroyed the Spanish Pacific fleet.

events.title: Treaty of Paris
events.description: Spain ceded the Philippines, Puerto Rico and Guam; the US became a colonial power.

conflicts.title: US Invasion of the Dominican Republic
conflicts.description: The US sent 42,000 troops to the Dominican Republic to crush a pro-democracy uprising trying to restore the elected president Juan Bosch, fearing 'another Cuba.' Thousands of Dominicans died. It was part of a long pattern of US interventions in the Caribbean.

events.title: US invades the Dominican Republic
events.description: US troops intervened in a civil war to prevent "another Cuba".

conflicts.title: Greek War of Independence
conflicts.description: Greeks rose against three centuries of Ottoman rule. Both sides committed massacres (Chios, Tripolitsa); the Ottomans' atrocities drew European 'philhellene' sympathy, and Britain, France, and Russia intervened, destroying the Ottoman-Egyptian fleet at Navarino. Greece won independence in 1832 — the first nation to break from the Ottoman Empire.

events.title: Uprising against Ottoman rule
events.description: Greeks rose against three centuries of Ottoman rule.

events.title: Chios massacre
events.description: Ottoman forces killed or enslaved most of the island’s population.

events.title: Battle of Navarino
events.description: A European fleet destroyed the Ottoman navy, securing Greek independence.

events.title: Greek independence recognised
events.description: Greece became an independent kingdom.

conflicts.title: Ottoman–Safavid Wars
conflicts.description: More than a century of wars between the Sunni Ottoman Empire and Shia Safavid Persia over Mesopotamia and the Caucasus, sharpening the Sunni–Shia divide. The Treaty of Zuhab (1639) fixed a border between Turkey/Iraq and Iran that still roughly holds today.

conflicts.title: Portuguese Colonial War
conflicts.description: Portugal fought to keep its African colonies — Angola, Mozambique, and Guinea-Bissau — against independence movements for 13 years. Atrocities like the Wiriyamu massacre occurred. The unwinnable war exhausted Portugal and triggered the 1974 Carnation Revolution at home, which finally freed the colonies.

events.title: War begins in Angola
events.description: Independence wars erupted across Portugal’s African colonies.

events.title: Carnation Revolution ends the war
events.description: A coup in Portugal led to independence for its colonies.

conflicts.title: Italian Colonization of Eritrea & Somalia
conflicts.description: Italy colonized Eritrea and Italian Somaliland from the 1880s, using forced labour and racial segregation, before briefly forming 'Italian East Africa' with occupied Ethiopia (1936). Italian rule ended with defeat by the Allies in WW2.

conflicts.title: Eritrean War of Independence
conflicts.description: Eritrea fought a 30-year war against Ethiopian annexation — one of Africa's longest wars. Ethiopia, backed first by the US then heavily by the USSR, used famine and bombing as weapons. An estimated 150,000+ died before Eritrea won independence in 1991 (formalized 1993).

events.title: Armed struggle begins
events.description: A 30-year war for independence from Ethiopia began.

events.title: EPLF captures Asmara
events.description: Eritrea won de facto independence, made formal in 1993.

conflicts.title: First Sudanese Civil War
conflicts.description: Even before independence, southern Sudan rose against domination by the Arab-Muslim north. The 17-year war killed an estimated 500,000, mostly civilians. A 1972 autonomy deal paused it — but its collapse reignited the even deadlier Second Sudanese Civil War in 1983.

events.title: Torit mutiny sparks the war
events.description: A southern mutiny began Africa’s longest civil war.

events.title: Addis Ababa Agreement
events.description: The accord granted the south autonomy for a decade.

conflicts.title: Sino-Indian War
conflicts.description: China and India fought a brief, sharp war over their disputed Himalayan border (Aksai Chin and Arunachal Pradesh). China won decisively. The border remains militarized and undefined, with deadly clashes as recently as 2020 (Galwan Valley).

events.title: China attacks Indian border positions
events.description: A short, sharp war over disputed Himalayan frontiers.

events.title: China declares a ceasefire
events.description: China held Aksai Chin; the border remains disputed.

conflicts.title: Sino-Vietnamese War
conflicts.description: China invaded northern Vietnam to 'punish' it for toppling China's ally the Khmer Rouge in Cambodia. Both sides claimed victory after a bloody month that killed tens of thousands; China withdrew, and border skirmishes continued through the 1980s.

events.title: China invades northern Vietnam
events.description: China attacked to 'punish' Vietnam over Cambodia.

events.title: Chinese withdrawal
events.description: Both sides claimed victory in a costly month-long war.

conflicts.title: Kurdish–Turkish Conflict (PKK)
conflicts.description: The Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) has waged an insurgency for Kurdish rights and autonomy against Turkey since 1984, killing an estimated 40,000+. Turkey has bombed Kurdish areas in Iraq and Syria. The Kurds — a stateless people of ~30 million split across Turkey, Iraq, Syria, and Iran — are among the largest ethnic groups without a state.

conflicts.title: West Papua Conflict
conflicts.description: Indonesia took over the former Dutch colony of West Papua via a widely-criticized 1969 'Act of Free Choice' (just 1,026 hand-picked voters). A low-level independence insurgency continues; rights groups estimate 100,000+ Papuans have died under Indonesian rule, with ongoing repression and restricted access for journalists.

events.title: Indonesia takes control of West Papua
events.description: Indonesia assumed control of the former Dutch territory.

events.title: "Act of Free Choice"
events.description: A vote by ~1,000 hand-picked elders formalised Indonesian rule.

conflicts.title: Aceh Conflict
conflicts.description: The Free Aceh Movement fought for independence from Indonesia for nearly 30 years, with an estimated 15,000+ killed amid army abuses. The devastating 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, which killed ~170,000 in Aceh, paradoxically pushed both sides to a 2005 peace deal granting autonomy.

events.title: Free Aceh Movement declares independence
events.description: A separatist insurgency began in resource-rich Aceh.

events.title: Indian Ocean tsunami devastates Aceh
events.description: The disaster killed ~170,000 in Aceh and pushed both sides to peace.

events.title: Helsinki peace agreement
events.description: The deal ended nearly 30 years of conflict.

conflicts.title: Moro Conflict (Philippines)
conflicts.description: Muslim Moro groups fought for self-rule in the southern Philippines (Mindanao) for half a century, with an estimated 120,000+ killed. A 2014 peace deal created the autonomous Bangsamoro region (ratified 2019), though splinter jihadist groups remain active.

events.title: Moro insurgency begins in Mindanao
events.description: Muslim Moro groups fought for autonomy in the southern Philippines.

events.title: Bangsamoro peace agreement
events.description: A deal created an autonomous Bangsamoro region.

conflicts.title: Philippine Drug War
conflicts.description: President Rodrigo Duterte's 'war on drugs' encouraged police and vigilantes to kill suspected drug users and dealers without trial. Official figures cite ~6,000 killed; rights groups estimate 12,000–30,000. The ICC opened a crimes-against-humanity investigation, and Duterte was arrested on an ICC warrant in 2025.

conflicts.title: Haiti Gang Crisis
conflicts.description: After the 2021 assassination of President Moïse, heavily-armed gangs seized control of over 85% of Port-au-Prince. More than 11,000 people were killed between 2024 and late 2025 and 1.4 million displaced. A Kenyan-led multinational force (2024) failed to stem it and was replaced by a UN-backed Gang Suppression Force in 2026. The crisis is fueled by weapons trafficked from the US.

conflicts.title: Red Sea Crisis (Houthi Attacks)
conflicts.description: In solidarity with Gaza, Yemen's Houthis began attacking commercial ships in the Red Sea and Bab el-Mandeb, sinking vessels and forcing much global shipping to reroute around Africa. The US and UK launched repeated airstrikes on Yemen (Operations Prosperity Guardian / Rough Rider). Attacks paused with the 2025 Gaza ceasefire but the Houthis vowed to resume after the 2026 US–Israel strikes on Iran.

conflicts.title: Portuguese Colonization & Slavery in Brazil
conflicts.description: Portugal colonized Brazil and made it the largest slave society in the Americas: roughly 5 million enslaved Africans were shipped there — about 40% of the entire transatlantic slave trade. Indigenous peoples were enslaved, killed, and devastated by disease. Brazil was the last country in the Western Hemisphere to abolish slavery, in 1888.

conflicts.title: Expulsion of Jews & Muslims from Spain
conflicts.description: In 1492 Spain expelled its Jews (the Alhambra Decree) — up to 200,000 forced to convert or flee — and the Inquisition persecuted those who remained. Muslims were forcibly converted then expelled (the Morisco expulsion, 1609–1614 drove out ~300,000). It was one of the largest religious cleansings in European history.

conflicts.title: Túpac Amaru II Rebellion
conflicts.description: The largest indigenous uprising against Spanish rule in the Americas, led by Túpac Amaru II in Peru against colonial forced labour and tribute. Spain crushed it brutally — executing the leader by dismemberment in the plaza of Cusco — and an estimated 100,000 died. It became a lasting symbol of indigenous and anti-colonial resistance.

events.title: Túpac Amaru II leads indigenous revolt
events.description: The largest indigenous uprising in colonial Spanish America.

events.title: Execution of Túpac Amaru II
events.description: The leader was brutally executed in the main square.

conflicts.title: Spanish American Wars of Independence
conflicts.description: Across two decades, most of Spanish America won independence from Spain in wars led by figures like Simón Bolívar and José de San Martín. The wars freed today's Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Peru, Bolivia, Chile, Argentina, and more, ending three centuries of Spanish colonial rule and reshaping the continent.

conflicts.title: Argentine Conquest of the Desert
conflicts.description: Argentina's military campaign to seize Patagonia from its indigenous peoples (Mapuche, Tehuelche, Ranquel) killed thousands, enslaved survivors, and shipped children to forced labour. It opened vast lands for European settlers and is widely described as a genocide that nearly erased Patagonia's native population. Chile waged a parallel 'Pacification of Araucanía'.

events.title: Argentine Conquest of the Desert
events.description: A military campaign seized Indigenous lands, killing and displacing thousands.

conflicts.title: Chaco War
conflicts.description: Bolivia and Paraguay fought the bloodiest South American war of the 20th century over the Gran Chaco region, wrongly believed to hold oil. Around 100,000 died, many from disease and thirst in the harsh terrain. Paraguay won most of the disputed land; both impoverished nations were devastated.

events.title: War erupts over the Gran Chaco
events.description: The deadliest South American war of the 20th century, over barren scrubland.

events.title: Ceasefire
events.description: Paraguay held most of the disputed territory.

conflicts.title: Cuban Revolution
conflicts.description: Fidel Castro and Che Guevara's guerrilla movement overthrew the US-backed dictator Batista in 1959, establishing a communist state 90 miles from Florida. It triggered the US embargo, the Bay of Pigs, and the 1962 Missile Crisis, and made Cuba a Cold War flashpoint and exporter of revolution.

events.title: Attack on the Moncada Barracks
events.description: Castro's failed assault launched the 26th of July Movement.

events.title: Granma landing
events.description: Castro and Guevara landed to begin the guerrilla war in the Sierra Maestra.

events.title: Castro takes power
events.description: Batista fled and the revolutionaries entered Havana.

conflicts.title: Cristero War (Mexico)
conflicts.description: A Catholic uprising against the Mexican government's violent enforcement of anti-clerical, secularist laws. Around 90,000 died before a US-brokered truce. Persecution of Catholics continued for years afterward.

events.title: Cristero War begins
events.description: Catholics rose against the anti-clerical government.

events.title: Settlement ends the war
events.description: A US-brokered deal ended a war that killed ~90,000.

conflicts.title: Canada — Residential Schools & Indigenous Genocide
conflicts.description: For over a century Canada forcibly removed Indigenous children to church-run 'residential schools' to erase their culture, where many suffered abuse, malnutrition, and death; thousands of unmarked graves have since been found. A 2015 Truth and Reconciliation Commission called it 'cultural genocide'. Combined with earlier dispossession and disease, it devastated First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples.

conflicts.title: Winter War
conflicts.description: The Soviet Union invaded Finland in 1939; vastly outnumbered Finnish forces inflicted huge losses in the snow before being forced to cede territory. The Soviets' poor performance encouraged Hitler to later invade the USSR. Finland kept its independence.

events.title: Soviet Union invades Finland
events.description: Finnish forces inflicted heavy losses on a far larger Soviet army.

events.title: Moscow Peace Treaty
events.description: Finland ceded territory but kept its independence.

conflicts.title: Katyn Massacre
conflicts.description: The Soviet secret police executed around 22,000 Polish military officers, police, and intellectuals in the Katyn forest and elsewhere, aiming to decapitate Poland's leadership class. The USSR blamed the Nazis and denied it for 50 years, only admitting responsibility in 1990.

events.title: Katyn massacre
events.description: The Soviets executed some 22,000 Polish officers and intellectuals.

conflicts.title: Soviet Deportation of Nations
conflicts.description: Stalin forcibly deported entire ethnic groups — Crimean Tatars, Chechens, Ingush, Volga Germans, Kalmyks, and others — to Central Asia and Siberia, accusing them of collective disloyalty. Packed into cattle trains, an estimated quarter to nearly half of some peoples died of cold, hunger, and disease. The Crimean Tatar deportation is recognized by some as genocide.

conflicts.title: Kazakh Famine (Asharshylyk)
conflicts.description: Stalin's forced collectivization and seizure of livestock caused a catastrophic famine in Kazakhstan that killed an estimated 1.5 million Kazakhs — around 40% of the entire Kazakh population, the highest death ratio of any group in the Soviet Union. The traditionally nomadic Kazakhs were made a minority in their own land. Many call it a genocide.

events.title: Forced collectivisation in Kazakhstan
events.description: Soviet collectivisation destroyed nomadic herding.

events.title: The Asharshylyk famine peaks
events.description: Around 1.5 million died — a huge share of the Kazakh population.

conflicts.title: Mongolian Stalinist Purges
conflicts.description: Under Soviet direction, Mongolia's leader Khorloogiin Choibalsan purged 'enemies of the people' — executing an estimated 30,000–35,000, a huge share of a small population. Buddhist monasteries were destroyed and tens of thousands of monks killed, all but erasing organized Buddhism in Mongolia for decades.

conflicts.title: Basmachi Revolt (Central Asia)
conflicts.description: A long Muslim uprising across Central Asia against Russian Imperial then Soviet rule, sparked by the 1916 conscription revolt. The Red Army crushed it over nearly two decades, killing an estimated hundreds of thousands and consolidating Soviet control over today's Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Turkmenistan.

events.title: Central Asian revolt against Russia
events.description: Revolt against conscription grew into a long anti-Soviet insurgency.

events.title: Soviet suppression of the Basmachi
events.description: The Red Army gradually crushed the resistance.

conflicts.title: Black January (Azerbaijan)
conflicts.description: Soviet troops stormed Baku in January 1990, killing around 130+ civilians to crush Azerbaijan's growing independence movement. It shattered any remaining Azerbaijani loyalty to Moscow and is commemorated as a national day of mourning.

events.title: Soviet crackdown in Baku
events.description: Soviet troops killed over a hundred civilians in Azerbaijan’s capital.

conflicts.title: Andijan Massacre (Uzbekistan)
conflicts.description: Uzbek government forces opened fire on a large crowd of mostly peaceful protesters in Andijan, killing an estimated several hundred to ~1,500. The regime of Islam Karimov sealed off the city and denied wrongdoing. It stands as one of the worst state massacres in post-Soviet Central Asia.

events.title: Andijan massacre
events.description: Uzbek troops fired on protesters, killing hundreds.

conflicts.title: Kyrgyz–Uzbek Ethnic Clashes (Osh)
conflicts.description: Ethnic violence between Kyrgyz and the Uzbek minority erupted in southern Kyrgyzstan (Osh and Jalal-Abad) after a political upheaval, killing around 400–2,000 and displacing hundreds of thousands. It echoed an earlier round of Osh clashes in 1990.

events.title: Ethnic violence in Osh
events.description: Clashes between Kyrgyz and Uzbeks killed hundreds.

conflicts.title: Georgian Civil Wars (Abkhazia & South Ossetia)
conflicts.description: As the USSR collapsed, the breakaway regions of Abkhazia and South Ossetia fought Georgia with Russian backing, driving out around 250,000 ethnic Georgians (ethnic cleansing in Abkhazia). The conflicts froze into Russian-backed statelets — the same fault lines that erupted again in the 2008 Russo-Georgian War.

events.title: War in Abkhazia begins
events.description: Separatist war broke out in the newly independent Georgia.

events.title: Fall of Sukhumi
events.description: Abkhaz forces took the city; ethnic Georgians were expelled.

conflicts.title: Great Turkish War (Siege of Vienna)
conflicts.description: The failed Ottoman siege of Vienna in 1683 turned into a sixteen-year war in which a Holy League led by Habsburg Austria and Poland-Lithuania pushed the Ottomans out of Hungary. The Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) marked the first major Ottoman territorial loss and the turning point of Ottoman power in Europe.

events.title: Battle of Vienna
events.description: A relief army broke the Ottoman siege, turning the tide in Europe.

events.title: Treaty of Karlowitz
events.description: The Ottomans ceded Hungary — their first major territorial loss.

conflicts.title: Khmelnytsky Uprising
conflicts.description: A Cossack revolt against Polish-Lithuanian rule in Ukraine that founded a Cossack state and led, in 1654, to alliance with Russia. The war was accompanied by large-scale massacres of Jewish communities and Polish nobility, with tens of thousands killed.

conflicts.title: King Philip's War
conflicts.description: An uprising of Wampanoag, Narragansett and allied peoples against the expanding New England colonies, and one of the deadliest wars in American history relative to population. It broke Native power in southern New England; thousands died and many survivors were sold into slavery.

conflicts.title: Shimabara Rebellion
conflicts.description: A revolt of overtaxed peasants, many of them Christian converts, in south-western Japan. The Tokugawa shogunate crushed it and executed tens of thousands, then sealed Japan off from the outside world and drove Christianity underground for two centuries.

conflicts.title: War of the Austrian Succession
conflicts.description: A pan-European war triggered by Prussia's seizure of Silesia and challenges to Maria Theresa's inheritance of the Habsburg lands. It drew in France, Spain, Britain and others across Europe and the colonies before the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle largely restored the pre-war map.

events.title: Prussia invades Silesia
events.description: Frederick the Great's seizure of Silesia triggered a European war.

events.title: Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle
events.description: The peace largely restored the pre-war map.

conflicts.title: Nader Shah's Invasion of India
conflicts.description: The Persian ruler Nader Shah invaded the Mughal Empire and sacked Delhi in 1739. His forces massacred an estimated 30,000 inhabitants in a single day and carried off the imperial treasury, including the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond, hastening the Mughal collapse.

events.title: Battle of Karnal
events.description: Nader Shah routed the Mughal army in three hours.

events.title: Sack of Delhi
events.description: Persian troops massacred some 30,000 and looted the imperial treasury.

conflicts.title: Partitions of Poland
conflicts.description: In three partitions (1772, 1793, 1795) Russia, Prussia and Austria progressively annexed the entire Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, erasing a large European state from the map for 123 years until 1918.

events.title: First Partition
events.description: Russia, Prussia and Austria seized a third of the Commonwealth.

events.title: Second Partition
events.description: A further partition left a rump Polish state.

events.title: Third Partition erases Poland
events.description: Poland vanished from the map for 123 years.

conflicts.title: Indian Rebellion of 1857 (Sepoy Mutiny)
conflicts.description: A major uprising against the rule of the British East India Company, sparked by mutinying sepoys and spreading across northern and central India. Its brutal suppression, with widespread reprisal killings of civilians, ended Company rule and brought India under the direct control of the British Crown.

events.title: Mutiny erupts at Meerut
events.description: Sepoys rose against the East India Company and marched on Delhi.

events.title: Siege of Cawnpore
events.description: Sieges and massacres on both sides marked the rebellion’s bloodiest phase.

events.title: Rebellion crushed; Crown rule begins
events.description: The Crown took direct control of India from the Company.

conflicts.title: Java War (Diponegoro War)
conflicts.description: A five-year revolt led by Prince Diponegoro against Dutch colonial rule in Java. It was the costliest conflict of the Dutch East Indies: around 200,000 Javanese died, many from famine and disease, and it cemented Dutch control over the island.

events.title: Java War begins under Diponegoro
events.description: A revolt against Dutch rule became the costliest war of the Dutch East Indies.

events.title: Diponegoro captured
events.description: The prince was seized under a flag of truce; ~200,000 had died.

conflicts.title: Mfecane
conflicts.description: A period of widespread upheaval, warfare and forced migration among southern African peoples, associated with the rise of the Zulu kingdom under Shaka. Estimates of the dead range widely, up to one to two million, and the disruption reshaped the demography of the region on the eve of European expansion.

events.title: Rise of the Zulu under Shaka
events.description: Zulu expansion triggered mass upheaval across southern Africa.

events.title: Death of Shaka
events.description: Shaka was assassinated after transforming the region.

conflicts.title: First Italo-Ethiopian War
conflicts.description: Italy's attempt to colonise Ethiopia ended in decisive defeat at the Battle of Adwa in 1896, where Emperor Menelik II's army routed the invaders. It was the first time an African state defeated a European colonial power and secured Ethiopian independence for another forty years.

events.title: Battle of Adwa
events.description: Ethiopia routed the Italian invaders — a landmark African victory.

conflicts.title: Caucasian War
conflicts.description: A decades-long campaign of Russian conquest across the Caucasus, subduing the mountain peoples of the North Caucasus and the South Caucasian states. It ended with the mass expulsion of Circassians and others, and folded the region into the Russian Empire.

events.title: Russia begins conquest of the Caucasus
events.description: A decades-long campaign to subdue the mountain peoples.

events.title: Capture of Imam Shamil
events.description: The surrender of the resistance leader broke organised resistance.

conflicts.title: Sand War (Algeria–Morocco)
conflicts.description: A border war in October 1963, barely a year after Algerian independence, when Morocco pressed claims to the resource-rich Tindouf and Béchar regions of the Algerian Sahara. An OAU-brokered ceasefire ended the fighting, but the border dispute simmered for years and fed later rivalry over the Western Sahara.

events.title: Border clashes erupt over the Sahara
events.description: Moroccan claims to Tindouf and Béchar sparked fighting along the disputed frontier.

events.title: Fighting at Hassi Beida and Tinjoub
events.description: Clashes over remote desert posts marked the height of the war.

events.title: Ceasefire (OAU mediation)
events.description: African and Arab mediation ended the fighting, leaving the border unresolved.

conflicts.title: Syria — Post-Assad Sectarian Violence
conflicts.description: The fall of Assad in December 2024 gave way to waves of sectarian bloodshed under the new transitional government. In March 2025 roughly 1,400 Alawites were killed in massacres along the coast; in July 2025, Druze–Bedouin fighting in Suwayda killed hundreds and drew Israeli airstrikes on Damascus.

events.title: Massacres of Alawites on the coast
events.description: Around 1,400 people, mostly Alawite civilians, were killed by pro-government and allied militias.

events.title: Suwayda clashes; Israel strikes Damascus
events.description: Druze–Bedouin fighting killed hundreds and displaced tens of thousands; Israel bombed Damascus to protect the Druze.
